name: citation-authority-ribbon
description: Source-type differentiation badges on references section entries. Six fixed source classes — academic (A, blue) / regulator (R, green) / industry (I, warm-gray) / direct-source (D, teal) / news (N, cool-gray) / web-informal (W, outline). data-source-authority attribute is the canonical machine-readable surface. JSON-LD @type refinement on citation entries. Addresses Wikipedia's flat numeric references list weakness.
html:
1. A Klein, G. et al. seL4: Formal Verification of an OS Kernel. ACM SOSP 2009. sel4.systems ↑
  2. R National Instrument 51-102 — Continuous Disclosure Obligations. BCSC. bcsc.bc.ca ↑
  3. I Anthropic engineering blog. Contextual retrieval. 2024. ↑
aria: Badge is <span> with aria-label (never aria-hidden — informative, not decorative). Tab-order unchanged: only citation hyperlinks and backref arrow are focusable. Backref <a> returns reader to inline footnote. Colour is never sole differentiator: each badge carries single-letter glyph + aria-label.
ai_hint: data-source-authority on each <li> is the canonical machine-readable surface. JSON-LD emission attaches @type refinement (AcademicSource, RegulatorSource, IndustrySource, DirectSource, NewsSource, WebInformalSource) on each citation entry in TechArticle schema.
open_questions: Single-letter glyph (A/R/I/D/N/W) vs full-word tag ('Academic', 'Regulator') — substrate ratifies. Trade-off: single-letter preserves Wikipedia density floor; full-word is more accessible on first encounter.
Badge position — leading the entry text vs. outside-left-gutter. Leading is denser; left-gutter creates a clear scan column.
